# The original study {#sec-intro}

```{r}
#| include: false
source("_common.R")
```

## Research question

In a choice experiment, people pick repeatedly among products that differ in
their attributes and prices. The method is now standard for valuing food
quality, environmental goods, and new products, and it is often assumed to be
more incentive compatible than open-ended contingent valuation because it looks
more like ordinary shopping.

Lusk and Schroeder ask whether that assumption actually holds. Do the choices
people make, and the willingness-to-pay (WTP) numbers we back out of them, change
when the task is hypothetical rather than backed by real money? This is the
"hypothetical bias" question from the contingent valuation literature, asked
inside the choice-experiment setting. What makes the paper useful is that it does
not just ask whether bias exists; it tries to say which WTP measure is affected,
the marginal value of a quality change or the total value of a steak versus
buying nothing.

## Empirical design

The good is a 12-oz ribeye steak, offered under five quality or assurance labels:
Generic, Guaranteed Tender (GT), Natural, USDA Choice, and Certified Angus Beef
(CAB). A sixth option, "None", lets the respondent decline to buy, so each choice
is among six alternatives.

Each subject saw 17 scenarios. In every scenario the five steaks were offered at
prices that varied by design (from \$3.38 to \$9.00), and the subject picked one
steak or chose None. The full price design is in the paper's appendix and is
reproduced in the [Data](02-data.qmd) chapter.

The manipulation that matters is the treatment:

- In the *nonhypothetical* (real) treatment, subjects knew that one scenario
  would be drawn at random and would actually count: they would buy the steak
  they had chosen at the posted price. Scenario 17, where all five steaks cost
  the same \$5.63, was the binding scenario.
- The *hypothetical* treatment used the identical task, except no purchase ever
  happened.

For estimation the authors fit a series of discrete-choice models to the panel
of choices: a multinomial logit (MNL), a test of IIA based on the universal
("mother") logit, and three less restrictive models, namely heteroscedastic
extreme value (HEV), multinomial probit (MNP), and random parameters logit (RPL).
They compare the two treatments with the Swait–Louviere scale-and-preference test
and test WTP differences with the Poe combinatorial test. All of this was done in
LIMDEP.

## Key findings of the paper

The paper reports four main results. Hypothetical respondents choose the
no-purchase option less often and have higher WTP than the real-payment group, so
hypothetical bias is present. The error scale differs across treatments, and a
joint model rejects equality of preferences across the two groups. IIA is
violated for the pooled and hypothetical data, which is what motivates the richer
HEV, MNP, and RPL models. Finally, the bias shows up mainly in total WTP (a steak
versus buying nothing) rather than in the marginal premiums between quality
levels, which is the practically useful part of the result.

::: {.callout-note}
## Why this paper is good to replicate
Almost every headline number is a likelihood, a test statistic, or a WTP, so each
one can be recomputed from the raw choices. That means the hard part is rarely
deciding what to calculate. The hard part is matching LIMDEP's 2004 defaults in
present-day R, and that is where most of my discrepancies come from (see
[Discrepancies](08-discrepancies.qmd)).
:::
